coredata.dc:title: Single-chip horticultural LEDs enabled by greenly synthesized red-emitting carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Mai, Xuan-Dung
Nguyen, Sinh-Hung
Tran, Dai-Luat
Nguyen, Van-Quang
Nguyen, Van-Hao
coredata.dc:description: Light emitting diodes (LEDs) having the emission spectra that match the absorption regions of a target plant are strongly demanded for vertical farming. Herein, we demonstrate the use of red-emitting carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) as a phosphor to fabricate cost-effective horticultural LEDs. R-CQDs having chlorophyll molecules embedded in a carbogenic matrix were solvothermally synthesized using ethanol extract of bougainvillea leaves as a green carbon source. R-CQDs emit a narrow band maximizing at 670 nm originated from the chlorophyll centers when excited either the carbogenic matrix or embedded chlorophyll. R-CQD phosphor converts partially 410 nm light emitted from a LED chip to red and far red regions, thus offering cheaper, single – chip LEDs for agriculture lighting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Agriculture lighting
LEDs
Carbon quantum dots
Red-emitting
Green synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) Full range and b) blue range PL spectra of R-CQDs dissolved in ethanol. c) TEM image of R-CQDs. d) Absorption and excitation spectra of R-CQDs; inset: pictures of R-CQD solution in ethanol or silicone under UV lamp (365 nm) or sunlight (middle). e) A tentative model explaining the formation and PL mechanism of R-CQDs.
A) Schematic structure and b) the emission spectrum of a horticultural LED using R-CQDs as a light-converting material.

head.ce:title: Single-chip horticultural LEDs enabled by greenly synthesized red-emitting carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of chemistry, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, Phuc Yen, Vietnam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of chemistry, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2, Phuc Yen, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Science and Technology, TNU University of Sciences, Thai Nguyen, Vietnam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Science and Technology, TNU University of Sciences, Thai Nguyen, Vietnam

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ethanol extract of bougainvillea leaves was carbonized by a solvothermal method to synthesize red-emitting carbon quantum dots (R-CQD) in which embedded chlorophyll are red-emitting centers.

Light emitting diodes (LEDs) having the emission spectra that match the absorption regions of a target plant are strongly demanded for vertical farming. Herein, we demonstrate the use of red-emitting carbon quantum dots (R-CQDs) as a phosphor to fabricate cost-effective horticultural LEDs. R-CQDs having chlorophyll molecules embedded in a carbogenic matrix were solvothermally synthesized using ethanol extract of bougainvillea leaves as a green carbon source. R-CQDs emit a narrow band maximizing at 670 nm originated from the chlorophyll centers when excited either the carbogenic matrix or embedded chlorophyll. R-CQD phosphor converts partially 410 nm light emitted from a LED chip to red and far red regions, thus offering cheaper, single – chip LEDs for agriculture lighting.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: LEDs have been employed increasingly in horticultural lighting due to a number of advantages such as low-energy consumption, environmental friendliness, low-heat emission, and versatile emission spectrum [1,2]. Light in different regions, ranging from 300 nm to 800 nm, contributes to different aspects of the plant photosynthesis [3]. Briefly, far red light (700– 800 nm) increases plant flowering and light harvesting by regulating the leaf angle and enhancing the leaf area [4,5]. Light in the ultraviolet (UV) region could induce an increase in UV shielding components and antioxidants such as flavonoids and phenolic compounds [6]. Light in the middle of the visible region exhibits a lower elongation phenomenon or shading response although it is negligibly absorbed by chloroplasts [3]. Blue light and red light have the most influences on the photosynthesis because common photoreceptors including chlorophyll A and B, β-carotene and phytochrome absorb efficiently light in those region [7–9].

The facile tunability of LEDs in terms of emission wavelength and color ratio could be achieved by combination of individual monochromic LEDs. This LED configuration has a high installation cost because it requires a great number of LED chips. Additionally, it also has a strong variation in blue to red irradiance according to the observed angle giving rise to inhomogeneous light exposure of different plants [1]. For cutting the price of horticultural LEDs dual blue- and red- emitting LEDs using single blue LED chip are strongly demanded and have urged the development of red-emitting phosphors (REP) recently [3]. Among considerable REP including organic dyes [10,11], inorganic matrixes doped with metal ions [12–14], and quantum dots (QDs), QDs have advantages in a broad excitation band and facile color tunability [3,15,16]. However QDs are less examined as REP in horticultural LEDs probably because of difficulties in mass production to lower the price of QDs [17]. Therefore, R-CQDs, especially R-CQDs derived from biomass resources that are low-cost and non-toxicity have a great potential in horticultural lighting application. Herein, we have demonstrated the use of R-CQDs that were derived from bougainvillea leaves as a red and far-red phosphor in horticultural LEDs.

10 g of bougainvillea leaves were collected from local area, washed sequentially with water and ethanol (96%), and soaked in 35 ml ethanol (99.8%) for overnight. The ethanol extract was heated at 180 °C for 4 h in a hydrothermal reactor. After being cooled naturally, the resultant mixture was filtered through 0,22 μm syringe filters and further dialyzed against ethanol (96%) for 24 h using a dialysis membrane with a molecular cutoff of 2000 Da to obtain R-CQDs. Horticultural LEDs were fabricated by coating a blend of R-CQDs and silicone (Dow Corning OE-7340) on the top of LEDs (1 W, JINGZHONGLED). UV-2450 (Shimadzu) and FLS1000 (Edinburgh instrument) spectrometers were used to conduct absorption and photoluminescent (PL) spectra of R-CQDs, respectively. The emission spectra of LEDs were obtained on a GS-1290 spectroradiometer (Gamma Scientific).

PL spectra of R-CQDs shown in Fig. 1 a exhibit a low-intensity band in the blue region and a broad emission band ranging from 600 nm to 775 nm in which a narrow emission peak with a FWHM of about 28 nm (or 73.4 meV) appeared at 670 nm. The blue region that red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength (Fig. 1b) could originate from a carbogenic matrix [18,19] which can be seen as dark dots in TEM images in Fig. 1c. A photoluminescent excitation spectrum observed at 670 nm was similar to the absorption profiles of R-CQDs dispersed either in ethanol or silicone, Fig. 1d, and exhibits some resolvable peaks at 500 nm, 533 nm, and 609 nm in addition to two dominant excitation bands peaking at 400 nm and 663 nm (Fig. S1). The peaks at 609 nm and 663 nm could be assigned to chlorophyll while the peaks at 500 nm and 533 nm were attributed to betalain dyes [20,21]. Because R-CQDs were purified by dialysis chlorophyll and betalain dyes were likely retained in the carbogenic core of R-CQDs formed via carbonization processes in a mechanism being similar to that reported for citric derived CQDs [17]. Chlorophyll embedded in carbogenic matrix accounts for the red-NIR emission of R-CQDs mentioned above or Zn-doped CQDs [22]. Importantly, the carbogenic matrix protects the chlorophyll emitting centers from photobleaching giving rise to gradual decrease in emission efficiency upon UV exposure (Fig. S2). Finally, the highest-intensity excitation peak at 400 nm could be attributed to the carbogenic matrix [18].

We put a tentative model in Fig. 1e to explain the formation as well as the optical origins in R-CQDs as following. Upon hydrothermal conditions, ethanol extract which include chlorophyll and betalain dyes, and many other molecules, oligomers underwent carbonization processes forming carbogenic structures with a diameter ranging from 2 to 7 nm (Fig. 1c). Some chlorophyll molecules could be unconverted and retained within carbogenic core of R-CQDs accounting for the resolved adsorption and excitation peak at about 663 nm discussed in Fig. 1d. When R-CQDs were excited high-energy excitons partially recombine in carbogenic matrix to emit blue light whose wavelength red-shifted with the excitation wavelength as discussed in Fig. 1b. Most of excitons likely migrate to the embedded chlorophyll within the same R-CQD where they recombine to emit light at 670 nm. Even if R-CQDs were excited by wavelengths ranging from 430 nm to 490 nm the emission at 670 nm was dominant over the expected yellow emission of betalain dyes [23], Fig. S3.

Because the red emission of R-CQDs originates from chlorophyll embedded in the carbogenic core as discussed above R-CQDs could emit intensively even in solid state, Fig. S4. When dispersed in silicone encapsulant R-CQDs retained their absorption profile and red emitting ability as shown in Fig. 1d and Fig. S5. By adjusting the concentration of R-CQDs or the thickness of composite over a LED whose structure is shown in Fig. 2 a we were able to obtain horticultural LED prototype having comparable emission intensities originated from the LED chip and R-CQDs phosphor as seen in Fig. 2b. Upon bumping by a LED chip at 410 nm R-CQDs emitted broadly ranging from 650 nm to about 800 nm. Therefore, horticultural LEDs demonstrated herein are suitable for plants for that red and near infrared lights are essential, Fig. S6.

R-CQDs with a diameter ranging from 2 to 7 nm were greenly synthesized from ethanol extract of bougainvillea leaves by a solvothermal method. Chlorophyll molecules that were partially retained and involved into carbogenic core of R-CQDs during the carbonization processes act as exciton focusing and emitting centers giving rise to a narrow PL peak at 670 nm. LEDs fabricated by using LED chips emitting at 410 nm and R-CQDs as a phosphor material emit light in blue and red – far red regions. Results demonstrated herein suggest low-cost horticultural LEDs for plants for which red and far red lights are important.

Xuan-Dung Mai: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Software. Sinh-Hung Nguyen: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Dai-Luat Tran: Formal analysis, Validation, Visualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Van-Quang Nguyen: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Van-Hao Nguyen: Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by Vietnam Ministry of Education and Training, Hanoi Pedagogical University 2 under project no. B.2022-SP2-04.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2023.134195.

ce:section.ce:para:
